STAR_TRAILS.rationale: 10-minute single exposure or stacked shorter frames. f/4 keeps stars sharp without diffraction artifacts.
STAR_TRAILS.proTip: Stack 60x 1-minute exposures instead of a single 60-minute frame to reduce sensor noise and heat buildup.
STAR_TRAILS.commonMistake: Attempting a single 10-minute exposure without an intervalometer leads to overheating and hot pixels.

MILKY_WAY_WIDE.rationale: 500 Rule at 24mm allows 20s before star trailing. f/2.8 maximizes light collection. ISO 3200 balances noise vs. signal.
MILKY_WAY_WIDE.proTip: Use an app like PhotoPills to plan Milky Way core position. New moon nights are essential for dark skies.
MILKY_WAY_WIDE.commonMistake: Shooting during a full moon or near city lights washes out the Milky Way completely.

MILKY_WAY_CLOSEUP.rationale: Shorter focal length (50mm) means shorter max shutter. Higher ISO and faster aperture compensate.
MILKY_WAY_CLOSEUP.proTip: Use a tracking mount to allow longer exposures at lower ISO for cleaner, more detailed star images.
MILKY_WAY_CLOSEUP.commonMistake: Exceeding the star trailing threshold creates elongated stars that look amateur and are hard to fix in post.

MOON_SURFACE_DETAIL.rationale: Sunny f/16 rule equivalent for a bright full moon. Low ISO and fast shutter for maximum detail.
MOON_SURFACE_DETAIL.proTip: A quarter moon shows more crater detail than a full moon—the oblique light creates strong relief shadows.
MOON_SURFACE_DETAIL.commonMistake: Overexposing the moon blows out crater detail. Start with f/11 at 1/125s and adjust.

NORTHERN_LIGHTS.rationale: Balance between capturing aurora movement and sharpness. Very active auroras need shorter shutters (2–4s).
NORTHERN_LIGHTS.proTip: Monitor KP index apps for aurora alerts. Active geomagnetic storms (KP4+) produce the strongest displays.
NORTHERN_LIGHTS.commonMistake: Long exposures during active aurora blur the moving curtains into a green blob without structure.

PLANETARY_PHOTOGRAPHY.rationale: Telescope + camera combination. Short exposure freezes atmospheric turbulence. Stack hundreds of frames in software.
PLANETARY_PHOTOGRAPHY.proTip: Use video mode to capture thousands of frames, then stack the sharpest 10% using AutoStakkert for incredible detail.
PLANETARY_PHOTOGRAPHY.commonMistake: Relying on a single long exposure for planetary photography—turbulence guarantees a blurry result.

METEOR_SHOWER.rationale: 30s intervalometer captures any meteor that streaks through. Wide angle covers a large sky area for maximum captures.
METEOR_SHOWER.proTip: Use an intervalometer with no gap between frames (bulb shooting). Point toward the radiant point for shorter but more visible trails.
METEOR_SHOWER.commonMistake: Leaving gaps between frames misses meteors—set intervals to 0 seconds between shots.

COMET_PHOTOGRAPHY.rationale: Comets require tracking mounts to follow their motion. Multiple stacked frames build up the tail detail.
COMET_PHOTOGRAPHY.proTip: Track on the comet nucleus itself—this keeps the coma and tail sharp while stars trail, emphasizing the comet.
COMET_PHOTOGRAPHY.commonMistake: Tracking on star field while trying to photograph a comet motion causes the comet itself to trail.

DEEP_SKY_OBJECTS.rationale: DSO require a star tracking mount to allow long exposures without trailing. Multiple frames stacked for signal-to-noise.
DEEP_SKY_OBJECTS.proTip: Use a dedicated astrophotography camera or enable long-exposure noise reduction and stack 30+ frames in DeepSkyStacker.
DEEP_SKY_OBJECTS.commonMistake: Attempting deep sky objects without a tracking mount results in star trails that ruin the image.

CITYSCAPE_STARS.rationale: Balancing bright city lights against faint stars requires compromising exposure. Blend two exposures in post for best results.
CITYSCAPE_STARS.proTip: Shoot one exposure for the city at ISO 800, and a separate longer exposure for the stars, then blend in post.
CITYSCAPE_STARS.commonMistake: City light pollution makes stars nearly invisible—travel to darker skies for serious astrophotography.

ASTRO_BLACK_WHITE.rationale: Monochrome astro images emphasize star field density and nebula structure. Shoot RAW for maximum conversion control.
ASTRO_BLACK_WHITE.proTip: Adding a Ha filter dramatically improves contrast and cuts through light pollution for black and white astro shots.
ASTRO_BLACK_WHITE.commonMistake: Converting color astro images to B&W without tonal remapping loses the beautiful color contrast between nebula and stars.

ASTRO_REFLECTIONS.rationale: Still water reflecting the Milky Way is one of the most dramatic compositions in astrophotography.
ASTRO_REFLECTIONS.proTip: Scout the location during daylight. Still, high-altitude lakes with clear dark skies are ideal.
ASTRO_REFLECTIONS.commonMistake: Any breeze ruins the water reflection—check wind forecasts before driving to remote locations.

ASTRO_LANDSCAPE.rationale: Combining a compelling foreground (rocks, arches, trees) with the Milky Way. May require focus blending for fore-to-sky sharpness.
ASTRO_LANDSCAPE.proTip: Take two exposures—one focused on the foreground and one on the stars, then blend them in Lightroom.
ASTRO_LANDSCAPE.commonMistake: Shooting with autofocus and failing to re-focus on the stars after composing the foreground.

LIGHT_PAINTING.rationale: Long exposure captures moving light tool while the photographer illuminates a foreground subject.
LIGHT_PAINTING.proTip: Wear dark clothing and keep moving—if you pause, your body will leave a ghost image in the frame.
LIGHT_PAINTING.commonMistake: Using a flashlight that's too bright at close range creates blown-out hotspots. Use diffused or colored lights.

ECLIPSE_PHOTOGRAPHY.rationale: NEVER photograph partial solar eclipse without a certified solar filter—it will permanently damage your sensor and eyes.
ECLIPSE_PHOTOGRAPHY.proTip: Remove solar filter ONLY during totality. During partial phases, use ISO 200 f/11 1/500 WITH filter. Bracket widely.
ECLIPSE_PHOTOGRAPHY.commonMistake: Removing solar filter before totality or not replacing it immediately after can permanently blind you and destroy sensor.

MILKY_WAY_APS_C_CROP.rationale: APS-C crop factor (1.5-1.6x) shortens the safe shutter at any given focal length. On a 24mm lens: 500 / (24 x 1.6) = 13s maximum before star trailing — 12s for safety.
MILKY_WAY_APS_C_CROP.proTip: Use the NPF rule for precision: (35 x aperture + 30 x pixel_pitch) / focal_length_equivalent. On a 24MP APS-C sensor this gives ~10-12s at 24mm f/2.8.
MILKY_WAY_APS_C_CROP.commonMistake: Applying the 20-second rule from full-frame guides to an APS-C camera. This produces star trails on crop sensors. Always apply the crop factor to your maximum shutter calculation.

LUNAR_ECLIPSE_BLOOD_MOON.rationale: During totality the Moon dims by ~10,000x compared to a full moon. The existing ECLIPSE_PHOTOGRAPHY preset is correct for solar eclipses and partial phases, but completely wrong for the blood moon totality phase.
LUNAR_ECLIPSE_BLOOD_MOON.proTip: Expose for the red colour, not the bright limb edge. Use live histogram and adjust continuously as the Moon dims. During partial phases bracket every 30 seconds.
LUNAR_ECLIPSE_BLOOD_MOON.commonMistake: Using the same settings throughout the entire eclipse. Brightness changes 10,000x from full moon to totality — you must continuously adjust ISO and shutter as the eclipse progresses.

DEEP_SKY_UNTRACKED.rationale: For those without a tracking mount. Maximum ISO compensates for the limited 15s exposure. Stack 20-50 frames in post to reduce noise. Only bright nebulae near the galactic core are realistic targets without tracking.
DEEP_SKY_UNTRACKED.proTip: Stack using Sequator (free, Windows) or Siril (free, Mac/PC). 20 stacked untracked frames beats a single 5-minute tracked frame for noise reduction.
DEEP_SKY_UNTRACKED.commonMistake: Attempting 120s without tracking produces star trails, not nebulae. Invest in a star tracker (iOptron SkyGuider Pro, Sky-Watcher Star Adventurer) before attempting deep sky objects.
